title: Solutions, challenges and rising tensions in AI and mathematics - Nature Machine Intelligence
text:
Download PDF

Recent breakthroughs in mathematical research show that AI is transforming the field at a remarkable pace. In an open letter published this month, an international group of mathematicians argue that the field needs to remain a human endeavour.

In a speech at the 2024 International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) in Bath, UK, the renowned mathematician and Fields medallist Terence Tao outlined three ways in which machines are supporting mathematics: specialized machine learning algorithms for discovering mathematical relations, large language models (LLMs) as assistive tools, and software systems for automated proof verification. In the years since, these applications have advanced at notable speed, and recent successes for LLMs in maths reasoning have led to tensions in the field.The first topic is central to scientific machine learning, as mathematics is the universal language for understanding diverse phenomena across engineering disciplines and natural sciences. For example, partial differential equations (PDEs) describe how systems evolve over space and time, and substantial research has been devoted to developing methods for solving them in increasingly realistic settings.A few years ago, neural operators emerged as powerful deep-learning PDE solvers, starting a successful wave of research in this field1,2. Furthermore, in a recent breakthrough, Long et al. develop ‘free boundary’ neural operators to tackle the challenging problem of solving PDEs when the domain of the solution is unknown. In an accompanying News & Views piece, Constantinos Siettos emphasizes the importance of extending scientific machine learning methods to free boundary problems, as they can involve irreversible and catastrophic transitions. Realistic problems of this kind, in need of good models, include glacial ice melting under climate change and tumour growth during cancer therapy.The field of artificial intelligence (AI)-based mathematical reasoning has also made notable strides in the past few years. Breakthroughs began around 2024, with demonstrations of systems that can solve Olympiad-level maths problems. Google DeepMind presented AlphaProof3, a generalist formal mathematical reasoning model that incorporates LLM-based reasoning and reinforcement learning. The method achieved silver medal-level performance in that year’s Mathematical Olympiad, solving four out of six problems and falling one point short of the gold-medal range. In related work, Zhang et al. developed a neuro-symbolic approach called TongGeometry that can solve as well as propose geometry problems. The method solved all 30 problems in a Mathematical Olympiad geometry benchmark problem dataset. Moreover, several problems designed by TongGeometry were selected for national mathematical competitions in 2024.Most recently, an internal OpenAI reasoning model was used to disprove a central conjecture related to the 80-year-old unit distance problem, one of the most famous questions in combinatorial geometry posed by Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős4. What makes this result notable is not only that a major open problem has been resolved, but that the model appears to have pursued a different approach from those typically explored by human mathematicians. Around the same time, Google DeepMind reported that AlphaProof Nexus, a system based on AlphaProof, had resolved nine more open Erdős problems5.AI developments have transformed most scientific domains in the past two decades, but the pace is particularly high in mathematics, possibly because mathematical output can, in principle, be readily verified. However, workflows for checking and independently verifying output are not keeping pace with the increasing use of AI technology6. Moreover, although solving maths challenges can be an effective way for an AI company to showcase the capabilities of its tools, mathematics arguably involves more than simply producing a correct answer. Mathematicians pursue problems with the goal of sparking creativity, new ideas and techniques, and collaboration.Many mathematicians are enthusiastic about the potential of AI in their field, but they are also increasingly worried about the rise of AI ‘slop’, and the growing involvement of technology companies in mathematical research, which may introduce skewed incentives. In a recent open letter7, the Leiden Declaration, an international group of mathematicians, raises concerns about the overuse of AI, warning that it could undermine longstanding values in the field, including transparency in mathematical reasoning, rigorous and independent verification, and the proper attribution of credit. The authors put forward a set of principles and recommendations for mathematicians, policymakers and companies, emphasizing among others the need to disclose the use of AI tools and to ensure appropriate recognition of human contributors.The Declaration also formulates a deep and far-reaching issue that deserves serious attention in mathematics and beyond: the need to avoid the erosion of research autonomy. Although AI in mathematics offers considerable potential to enable major discoveries, researchers must retain the freedom to decide whether and how to incorporate AI into their work, as the Declaration points out.
